content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I just watched Downfall (2004) last night, and it was amazingly effective at showing how many Nazis were true believers to the very end.

**Score:** 224
**Body:** Downfall (2004) is a historical docudrama showing the final 10 days of World War II from the perspective of people in Adolf Hitler’s bunker. It’s very realistic, and it shows what the final days might have looked like when Hitler realized he would lose World War II.

It showed how vindictive and self destructive Hitler was, as well as how much backstabbing took place between his top Nazi generals. Towards the end, Hitler declared that he didn’t care if millions of civilians died, because “there are no non-combatants in total war.” He also blamed the German people for electing him, and he ordered Germany’s civilian infrastructure to be destroyed. He also believed that the non-Nazi survivors should be killed because they are weak compared to his Nazi supporters.

I think it’s an important film to watch because it shows just how demented evil people in power can be and what a cornered dog looks like in the last days of his life. It reminds me of some political leaders in charge today, too.

Highly recommend!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I would also recommend Japan's Longest Day which shows how many Japanese were true believers to the very end of WW2.

A little known history - there was actually a failed coup in Japan trying to stop Japan surrender after the atomic bombs.
2. That's an amazing movie. If you get the chance watch the extended cut it has 25 more intense minutes.  Bruno Ganz did a great job, the entire cast was excellent 
3. I remember when it was released people were really angry that it humanised Hitler.
  - The banality of evil.
4. The Zone of Interest is also a good film. It highlights how ordinary people were complicit in what happened during the war
5. What a film, and I appreciate the thoughts you shared. As I watched it, the current parallels in the US made my heart ache. When cornered, zealots REALLY start acting out, and you noted some of the actions in the film. I fear our lead zealot in the US has only begun his acting out. 
6. No respect for human life, no sense of basic survival. Running around demanding suicide, committing suicide, first shooting everyone they know, and killing their own children. Shocking to see that level of total absence of the survival instinct. The film was mostly about those in the bunker w hitler at the end, as i recall, not the general public. I understand the general public was all for surviving,  if they could. 
7. Yes, I was struck by how at the end it’s unthinkable to the characters that they might have to face a world without National Socialism.

Also, the fate of the Goebbels kids 🌚
  - Yes- the Goebbels family scene was the most horrifying scene. Frau Goebbels “lovingly” administered neurotoxins to her children, killing them in their sleep. It’s a brutal movie, but I think the brutality is important to witness, because it really actually happened.
  - That aspect was particularly disturbing.

## 2. Melancholia (2011)

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Not often do you see an ostensibly sci-fi disaster movie being so deeply empathetic to the experience of depression. It's an incredibly difficult task to depict a character feeling a "haunting" sense of peace as doom becomes a certitude. Lars von Trier executes this perfectly. 

Don't expect to see any of the usual tropes of the disaster genre as the film squarely relies on the exploration/observation of psychological, physical, and dramatic contrast between the two sisters to push the story forward. The impending end of the world is a mere catalyst. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fantastic film, one of my favorites.  Kirsten Dunst is incredible in this.  The beginning sequence is one of the best opening sequences ever imo.

Obviously a film about depression, and the planet slowly approaching is a metaphor for that (as if the planet being named "Melancholia" didn't give it away).  What I appreciate is how it shows everyone around the Kirsten Dunst character trying to cope with her depression.  Denial, anger, frustration, unfounded optimism, confusion, etc.  I think it represents in an artistic way what it feels like for family members of someone who is suffering from severe depression.  No matter how they each try to cope, nothing will stop it.  The complete destruction was always inevitable from the beginning (probably why von Trier basically gives away the ending in the opening sequence).
2. It's a very fine movie, one of my favorites, and while I could poke holes in the science of the rogue planet, that really doesn't matter - it's the depressed mood that the movie evokes and how those few people react to their inevitable doom that is so fascinating, it's a great way to depict depression. I also love the use of the classical music. 

Also, when the world actually feels like it's falling apart why not watch a movie depicting the planet quite literally falling apart?
3. Antichrist (2009) - Melancholia (2011) - Nymphomaniac (2013) are supposed to be a trilogy, each treating a different side of depression and mental health struggles, from someone who intimately associates with the topic. Each depiction is beautiful, and difficult to go through, in its own way. 
  - I loved Antichrist. Lars Von Trier has a wonderful way of immersing the viewer.  Dancer in the dark was a brilliant movie as well.
4. Tarkovsky was really good at making sci fi disaster movies that are deeply empathetic to the experience of depression, so I'd check those out, especially Solaris and Stalker.
5. Melancholia was the closest I've ever seen a movie come to replicating a weird, icky feeling, doom dream.
6. This is one of my all time favorites movies. Just beautiful. 
7. I got depressed watching this stupid movie.
  - Current events are dystopian, I don't need any help from movies.
8. I think I’ll revisit this movie today. I haven’t watched it for years as it’s a tough watch is you suffer from depression.

## 3. Movie set in the Bayou?

**Score:** 30
**Body:** Looking for some movies set in the Bayou/Louisiana. Any genre works! I’ve seen Sinners, Princess and the Frog, Eve’s Bayou, and Scooby Doo Zombie Island. Odd set of movies I know I just like the setting a lot 😂

Edit: Wow this got a lot more recommendations than I expected, thanks guys! I’m gonna try to check every one of them out if I can! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *Beasts of the Southern Wild* (2012) is definitely worth a look.
2. Waterboy
3. Southern Comfort (1981) Fantastic film
4. Angel Heart
5. The Rescuers
6. The Big Easy 
7. Skeleton key
8. Steel Magnolias

Passion Fish

Dead Man Walking
9. Angel Heart 

Interview with the Vampire

The Big Easy 

The Pelican Brief

Curious Case of Benjamin Button 

A Streetcar Named Desire




10. Part of the movie Double Jeopardy features several scenes (notably, one at a cemetery) in New Orleans

## 4. Movies with an important or iconic use of grass?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** As the title says, I'm looking for a movie where grass plays a unique and memorable role, either for some pivotal scene, or over the course of the movie. The movie does not have to literally be about grass, it just needs to have some relevant and interesting use for it. Any genre would be welcome. I've been bored and needed a way to find direction with which movies I watch next so I decided to find films where each focuses and makes use of a random mundane everyday thing, usually by looking through dictionaries. This time it's grass.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Honey I Shrunk The Kids
2. Edward Scissorhands. The grass lawns and gardens are a subversive thematic element subverting the concepts of 1950's conformity.
3. _Field of Dreams_
4. Up in smoke
5. The Happening
6. Surprised no one said Gladiator
7. In The Tall Grass
8. maybe the beginning of Blue Velvet 
9. Vivarium
10. Happy Gilmore

## 5. Best serial killer movies?

**Score:** 23
**Body:** Which serial killer movies do you think are worth a watch? I recently saw The House that Jack Built and it was a difficult watch but would recommend it. Any other good ones? I'm not a psycho don't judge just in the mood to watch that kind of thing tonight. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Henry: Portrait of a Serial Killer 
2. zodiac from 2007
3. Besides the obvious Silence of the lambs and Seven, I would go with Chained (2012)
4. I Saw The Devil, feel like this could fit here 
5. Clovehitch killer is good one
6. Memories of Murder
7. If you're more on the whimsical, deranged side of things, Seven Psychopaths is great.
8. spoorloos from 1988 is incredible if you don't mind subtitles
9. Kalifornia. See Brad Pitt play crazy. It's interesting and an early role for him
10. Henry: Portrait of a Serial Killer

The Hitcher

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. A movie with Zero flaws and Zero Haters(Starship Troopers)

**Score:** 2429
**Body:** Starship Troopers(1997)

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'd like to know more.
  - ![gif](giphy|u8Kvc4KRYHfqM)
    - ![gif](giphy|7JgYv9FobG1HzAO8BA)
      - ![gif](giphy|5L2okTiupVRxC)
2. Big Trouble In Little China.
3. Monty Python and the Holy Grail 
  - ![gif](giphy|53v5dZmDN5I1a)


4. Tombstone

5. TMNT 1990. It’s insane how they actually pulled it off and it’s amazing. 

## 2. Rewatched The Prestige and absolutely enjoyed every bit of it. Each conversation carries a lot of value for the story. Which Nolan film do you think has the most rewatchability?

**Score:** 805
**Body:** The conversations involving Nikola Tesla were fascinating. The nuances in every dialogue delivered by Bale were brilliant. Great acting from Michael Caine, and Hugh was also very good in it.

 I found this to be the most rewatchable Nolan film for me. What about you? Which Nolan movie do you rewatch the most?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. David Bowie as Tesla is the best Tesla on screen we got so far!
  - He's a great actor. As iconic as he is I never even realized it was him after watching the movie four times until someone pointed it out on reddit.
  - ![gif](giphy|vuQnywLv4nFNm)
2. The Prestige is my personal favorite. The actors are all A+, the costume/set design is immersive, and it's a prime example of a movie that is better the second watch, or third. 
  - Almost the entirety of Nolan’s career has been trying to replicate the trick he pulled off with The Prestige, which is fun as a bit of meta-commentary. 


    - following, memento, interstellar. Plus maybe inception. Dude has a ton of movies like that. 
  - My favorite Nolan film as well. I really like the setting, the characters, and the very small bits of sci fi thrown in. Its got that Nolan meta story telling thing going on as well but without being as convoluted or confusing as Tenet or even Inception.
3. I’ve probably seen this one the most, outside of the Batman Tril. Great film 
4. Ironically, not revealing the trick cost both of them everything. 
5. It’s a perfect movie a magic trick within a movie all about magic tricks. It’s has poetry, immense gravitas and the twist, well once you know, you may find you agree with me. Dark though, really dark.

## 3. Enough with The Godfather— Petition to make Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind as the benchmark cinema

6. Damn good movie
7. Enough with eternal sunshine of the spotless mind— petition to make the lord of the rings as the benchmark of cinema
8. I try to tell people about this movie, and hardly anyone has seen it.  This movie makes you feel stuff.  What more can you ask?

*The Princess Bride* is the only right answer

## 4. Best action actress

**Score:** 339
**Body:** 40 years ago Ellen Ripley kicked some serious ass in Aliens. But has any women in an action role come close to  topping her crown

![gif](giphy|dUCaihquaVMrj4TwTv)

I've watched the Godfather trilogy once, I've watch Eternal Sunshine five or six times... But Lord of the Rings? That's twice a year, minimum. I'm probably pushing close to 40 full watch throughs. *The Princess Bride*

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Bee Movie had a genuinely interesting premise… until it stopped following its own logic

**Score:** 215
**Body:** [](https://www.reddit.com/r/CharacterRant/?f=flair_name%3A%22Films%20%26%20TV%22)I rewatched Bee Movie recently, and what surprised me most wasn’t how weird it is, it’s how strong the setup actually is.

The first 30 minutes are genuinely creative. The movie establishes a structured bee society with rules, expectations, and even cultural taboos. One of the biggest ones is that bees do not talk to humans under any circumstances. It’s treated as a serious, almost unforgivable boundary.

Then Barry breaks that rule and that’s where the movie should really take off.

When he discovers that humans have been stealing honey, it feels like the story is building toward something meaningful. You’ve got a clear conflict: a bee realizing his entire species is being exploited, with only one human connection (Vanessa) to bridge that gap. There’s potential for tension, escalation, even some real commentary.

But instead of building on that foundation, the movie just… drops it.

Suddenly, bees are openly talking to humans. They’re in courtrooms, interacting freely, and the original “no talking” rule basically stops mattering. What was set up as a major boundary becomes irrelevant.

From there, the story shifts into a series of gags and absurd scenarios without the structure it originally built.

And that’s my main issue with the movie.

It’s not that it’s a “kids movie” or that it’s silly plenty of animated films balance humor with consistent storytelling. The problem is that Bee Movie introduces rules and stakes, then doesn’t follow through on them.

There’s still fun to be had, and I get why it became popular over time. But rewatching it now, it really feels like a movie that had the foundation for something sharper and just didn’t commit to it.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My main issue with the movie is that it’s capitalist propaganda lol. 

Like the bees realise they’re being exploited, fight to stop the exploitation, and win, so that they now don’t need to work much. And the result is they become lazy and unfulfilled, and ask for humans to keep taking their honey. 

It works pretty clearly as a metaphor for the value the workers create being stolen by company owners/shareholders. But the movie portrays that the end of this exploitation would mean everyone is bored because they don’t have to work anymore. It’s fucked lol. 

It’s not that weird that it’s anti worker/pro capitalist either, considering it was written by a billionaire (Seinfeld). 
  - This is a compeletely valid point, but I think the capitalist message was accidental. If anything, Barry's fight for worker's rights is taken very seriously for most of the runtime, I've seen some people say Bee Movie is socialist lol.

I think mainly Seinfeld was trying to make a pretty simple cookie cutter "We need to apprechiate the bees and makes sure they don't go extinct, think of how much we use their honey" message and then tried to make an absurdist comedy piece with pretty much free reign. 

When Bees are anthropromorphised and given human emotions, suddenly the film becomes far more allegorical, presumbably completely on accident, resulting in sending mixed messages thematically. 
    - Yeah I think it was probably accidental, but I do think it revealed the worldview of the writers, intentionally or not. Like it’s barely even metaphorical, it’s literal workers fighting to stop the fruits of their labour being stolen - they’re just bees lol 
    - I still can’t believe a kids’ movie has a talking bee saying the line: “Do you want to spend the rest of your lives as honey slaves for the *white man*?” 
  - Being a capitalist billionaire isn’t even the worst thing about Jerry Seinfeld.
2. I think you do have to consider this as a kids movie.

The one kids movie that did what you want was Over the Hedge. It was largely a flop among kids, but adults loved it.

So Bee movie stuck to the kids movie silliness and did pretty well among the kids.
  - What do you mean? Pixar movies follow through on their established premise and world-building more often than not. 
3. Agreed. There are many complex characters in anime, an underrated example being Vanessa Bloome from Bee Movie. I always liked to imagine myself as Vanessa cucking my husband for that beenis 😍
  - Hold up. 
  - Beenis is killing me 💀

## 2. Just watched Marty Supreme. My thoughts on it

**Score:** 55
**Body:** I know I'm late to the party, but glad I finally watched it. It is *very interesting.* In fact, I would go as far as to say that this is the best film of 2025 for me. How it was snubbed at the Oscars is beyond me. I think Chalamet as Mouser was nothing short of incredible, the nuance, the emotions, the drama, everything was so natural.

  
There's always an inherent tension and chaos in any Safdie film and this is no different. In fact, I would rate it at the same level as Uncut Gems. The main reason I really like it and relate to it because I myself have played a bit of professional table tennis in my college. And to say the least, all the table tennis scenes were done really well. Very happy to see the sport getting some representation in films as well.

  
Talking about the cinematography, it is special, especially that chase sequence when he tries to run with the bag of money from the policeman. The streets and the sets recreate the 50s New York atmosphere beatifully.

  
What I found the most interesting was the choice of the kind of music used in the soundtrack. Most of the tracks are soft rock or blues songs from the 80s/90s, event though the film is based in 50s. In the opening sex scene, Forever Young by Alphaville is played. I would never have thought to use a song like that in a sex scene, I found it a bit off-putting and unnatural, but *interesting.*

  
Finally, the ending. I think many people have misread the ending as Marty finally achieving his ambition and then coming back to see his wife & child thinking I have conquered the world. But I feel its exactly the opposite. The man is a huge narcissist, and will go to any length to get his game of table tennis against Endo (literally leaving his injured pregnant girlfriend in the hospital). But all the people he has done wrong, all the screw ups that he did, all the people he betrayed throughout to get the money for the championship, in the end it is all in vain. *He never gets to play the championship.* He just somehow half-assed his way to Japan, realizing in the end he will have to settle for just an exhibition match. And that shows in the end, after he arrives back at the hospital. We don't see his cocky demeanour instead he cries like a baby. That just shows the realizationt that he arrived at. The baby and the mother are the true meaning he is looking for. Not obsessive ambition. He had to literally beg his way to Japan (don't get me started on that Rockwell scene). But I think you do get the point.

  
Anyways, I think I made this too long. Lemme know what you guys thought of it, would love to hear some fresh takes.

  
P.S.

A24 is producing some absolute bangers since the comeback. Kudos

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think it's interesting to see it be received as so controversial. I think it's pretty clearly to some extent a comedy - Marty's a loser who achieves success in a tiny (pretty much meaningless) portion of his life and believes it to mean he's a broadly successful person. He endlessly manipulates others to regain that success once he loses it, yet in the process gains some appreciation for the people he once just manipulated. I think at the end he does realize his "success" - beating the Japanese champ at a sham game after the guy was playing table tennis literally all day long - doesn't mean much of anything, but the people who helped him get there do. By accepting responsibility for his child, Marty shows he accepts responsibility for the wellbeing of people other than himself. I feel like that's a pretty textbook example of character development that wasn't particularly subtle, so I'm curious why people take issue with it. Maybe everyone's just sick of wheeler dealers like Marty being portrayed as protagonists, which is understandable in 2026, when that archetype of American is pretty hellbent on destroying the world, but I also think the film clearly depicts the way he lives as immoral at best. Completely agree with you.
  - IMO a handful of people don’t like it because they associate it with MAGA, since I believe some of the crew and cast are right wing affiliated, not to mention the accusations against Safdie. This doesn’t include people that didn’t like it because it was stressful and the characters were unlikable

But I believe if you separate the art from the artist, it’s a great film. I personally enjoy anti-heroes and stressful movies, so it was for me. But not liking it for its political and social connotations is the same as the people that hated OBAA for having politically charged themes from the left, and painting far-right politicians as the enemy. Both films were excellent in more than one way, from their acting to their cinematography to their scripts. Even if it wasn’t your “type” or in line with your “taste” and you didn’t personally enjoy it, it doesn’t hold much weight IMO when people say they are bad, poorly executed films. 

There’s a difference between a bad film and a film you didn’t like. I wasn’t the biggest fan of OBAA (I did enjoy it, but it’s further down the list of PTA films for me), but I can still recognize it was an extraordinary work of art that well-deserved the best picture win. 
    - I was thinking about it maybe being right wing in a much more abstract way - as a valorization of the American hustler - but I forgot the billionaire elephant in the room! I agree with you too though, I still think it's a great film. Also an interesting example of how all art is political even if on its face it's just about a deadbeat who's nice at ping pong.
  - There is a lot of cognitive dissonance built into the movie because it uses the tropes of a postwar American sports hero but Marty is such a POS that it makes you realize he is an antihero. I do think you're smart to point out that we are tired of antiheros, but everything in the movie feels so triumphant at first glance that it's easy to think the movie is endorsing him. Shit even you think it ends with him accepting responsibility, when I think a valid read is that you seem him dreading it. Will Marty really be a good dad in 5 years time? I wouldn't bet the farm on it
    - What I don't understand about the read of "Marty can't change" is why they think we are supposed to assume this is a complete and total instant turnaround for him rather than the beginning of a shift in priorities. The ending in no way indicates that he's going to stop being a hustling rude little pissant, but it does seem to indicate that he might continue to be who he is in a slightly less self absorbed way going forward. I imagine he'll still be coming up with schemes left and right but now in pursuit of something less grandiose than "greatness." More focused on his real responsibilities than previously, but still tackling them in a frustratingly Marty way.
      - Yeah I'm not sure the film is taking a position on what Marty is going to do. This is just the first time he's being confronted with the existence of someone outside of himself in a way that really breaks through. It's the Call to Adventure in monomyth terms.

I think the movie is saying that Marty *can* change, and it wants him to. But that's different than saying that Marty *will* change.
        - > I think the movie is saying that Marty can change, and it wants him to. But that's different than saying that Marty will change.

I think that’s a great way of putting it
2. Personally I couldn’t get into it because it felt like Safdie was doubling down on the elements that made Good Time and Uncut Gems great, but without the restraint and focus that allowed the controlled chaos of those other films to shine. The stunt casting was too self aware and I frankly didn’t buy many of the performances from the leads. It felt like a disconnected sequence of montages rather than a coherent narrative. But a film like Licorice Pizza, which I thought delivered on the montage structure in a very successful way, had rich and interesting characters which carried the film. I was hoping for a step forward from Josh Safdie here but it felt like more of the same, but less successfully executed.
  - This is exactly my opinion, but said better than I managed to express in my comment. 
3. I was actually about to post about this film and ask whether he truly was a narcissist, or rather a very ego-driven, self-absorbed intelligent machiavellian and inexperienced young man who was still stuck in his youthful cockiness, with a childish “me” perspective. This question arose in part because in the movie, Rachel states that he is a narcissist and to me Im not sure that’s the case. I would like to hear if it’s actually correct to label him as such.

Marty lives a bit of a complex life, at the surface level he seems like he some support network, living with his mother and working for his uncle, but underneath it his mother seems manipulative at best and his uncle just wants to use him to have him stay in his shoe business because he’s a good worker. None of them really seems supportive for him to pursue his grand sport ambitions.

In regards to the music choices in this movie I think it’s brilliant, I think it’s funny because I absolutely loved the use and timing of that Alphaville "Forever young" song and found the use of it oddly surprising, but in a masterful way on how the intro is cut into the. I think Safdie was snubbed from the academy award this year of best director in this movie of all the details and nuances he put into this movie.

I loved the pace of the movie, thats the most refreshing aspect of it, and the at times surprising turn of events (SPOILER: >!Who foresaw that the old man was going to straight up stab the guy standing with his the Moses!<, although some of the plot you could see coming a mile away SPOILER: >!that the old man was going to get shot when trying to reclaim his dog!<) - I also think it was a masterful casting, great photography, great sets, great music choices. A great flick. Not perfect flick - but great nonetheless. I cant help but think that the movie could have become one of the top fantastical films of of the 2020's so far if perhaps he had involved his brother win tweaking and sharpening the script, the plot and the trajectory of it.

## 3. Trying to put into words what makes unsavory Safdie characters relatable. Please help in explaining why this is the case?

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Just watched Marty Supreme and I am trying to pin down what makes Safdie characters either relatable or eventually people to root for. I remember I had the same experience with Uncut Gems in the sense that I eventually rooted for the main characters' success.

However, I can't quite pinpoint why I'm rooting for them. Clearly, those characters are unsavory personalities who do not seem to be the kind of people we don't wanna meet in real life. And yet when we see them struggle, it's as if we are swept into thinking that they deserve a level of success in their fields.

I am just trying to understand what makes it all work. What is with these characters that eventually we are beaten down to cheering for them somehow despite their gazillion faults? It feels like some sort of je nes sais quois element that I feel is explainable. Thanks!

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love all their movies dearly, but I found it hard to actually root for the protagonists in Uncut gems, Good Time and Marty supreme - It's more like watching a car crash in slow motion and you can't look away
2. Maybe because deep down we are all deeply flawed people. 

A lot of people in the art space like to act morally righteous and show disdain for portrayals of less than ideal people in film and music, but I don’t believe this is healthy. 

All of us have a shadow side that we often choose to not reveal to those around us, or even to ourselves if we can handle it. Unsavoury characters like those in Safdie films provide a window to that side of humanity.
  - Thank you for this! You're right. I feel like even when most artworks (even the great ones) try not to be overtly didactic, they come across with "black-and-white" moralistic interpretations in showing deeply flawed characters as completely in the wrong. As if those characters cannot teach, or maybe even *doesn't deserve* to teach, us anything about life. 

I also like what you said about the shadow side, too. Because it shows how even those "shadow" characters can show us the "light".
3. They have a clear, intense dream and they are actively and unashamedly pursuing it. We all have dreams and wish we could pursue them, but we've allowed ourselves to abandon them for the sake of playing it safe or respecting other's feelings.

We hope to see in these characters what we could've achieved if we hadn't abandoned our own dreams.

This is why I think Marty is the "best" of the Safdie antiheroes: his dream is the most pure. He wants to prove to himself that he's the best table tennis player, and in the end, he does.

Howard (Sandler in Uncut Gems) wants to prove to himself that he's a winner, not a loser. Just when he's about to realize it, \[bad thing omitted to prevent spoilers\].

Connie (Pattinson in Good Time) wants to protect his brother. He never quite realizes that he's incapable of doing it.
  - I interpreted Marty's ambition was to be a "big shot" to feed his narcissism. Table tennis was just his avenue to get there. 

He pursued Kay for the same reason. He wasn't attracted to her as a person. She was famous, and bedding her would elevate his self-percieved status.
    - His dalliance with Kay was definitely that. He knew his ego would be boosted if he slept with a movie star even if said star already faded.
4. With Uncut Gems specifically, that was also due largely to how charismatic and charming Adam Sandler is. He has years and years of movies where he’s the lovable schlub, and he plays those parts well because he does have the vibe of a nice guy that is just trying his best. So, they cast him partially because he’s charismatic and likable enough to make you like the character even though he’s doing basically nothing redeemable. 

I think it’s just that they write good believable characters and then they can just cast well enough that you still root for them even when they’re basically purely selfish 
  - Timothy was a great choice for exactly this. Like, his dream is acting not ping-pong, but they both have this similar drive, plus the charisma/skill/talent to actually pull it off.  

Now all Timothee did was utter a couple of mildly cringey sentences about ballet and opera, versus fucking someone else’s wife then screwing up their big international business deal up.  But you can see the parallel?
5. I have referred to it as “trainwreck protagonists.” It’s maybe a bit similar to watching reality TV, but a big part of the difference to me is that we are being exposed to more of the characters and the “machinery” of the story in a Safdie movie. We’re seeing sides to characters that directors don’t normally show.

Even if the characters are in-character the entire time, it still feels like we’re seeing actors in the dressing room, or bartenders out back smoking. It makes the rest of the film feel more intimate.

Basically, a regular movie will show a character look in the mirror and steel themselves, preparing for something. But the camera composition is still looking at them “straight-on.” When the Safdies do a scene like that, I feel like they’ll let it resemble a student film; the camera will be hidden away, placed somewhere that it couldn’t be shown in the reflection with or without camera tricks, looking up at the character. It shows characters in the wake of whatever momentous thing they just did, without another character to play off of (thinking of Uncut Gems especially here). In Uncut Gems, we see Adam Sandler’s character “break character” by going from scummy to halfway-to-a-breakdown. 

They are not breaking the fourth wall, but they are allowing the characters to take off their masks in-universe; i.e., the sociological theory that everyone is acting all the time, but we see the characters take that smoke break on the ‘worst day of their life,’ where pretense is losing a lot of its uses.
6. Tenacity and momentum. The characters don’t give up no matter how much the odds increasingly stack up against them. They keep moving forward towards their goals despite finding themselves hitting insane headwinds opposing them and the Safdies write and direct the films to have a propulsive momentum that keeps us invested. The filmmaking all around has an intensity that keeps the tension extremely high. Obviously can be very anxiety inducing but never dull in my opinion.

## 4. General approach to what films you watch

**Score:** 8
**Body:** There's a lot of talk about specific films, artists etc. but what about the overall approach to cinema? There are thousands of films so how do you make your picks and what's your advice for others? We all follow our own unique path in experiencing art but I think that sharing our approaches can be useful to others.

Personally, I work on two "directions". The first and easier one, is to watch the best new films. Depending on free time, how much you enjoy them, whether it's a good film year etc. this can be 30 films or 100 per year but I think that's a good range. I prioritise new films firstly because I think the mastery on most technical aspects (like visual effects, sound) is at its peak now compared to the past and secondly, because the best time to experience a film is the closest to its release. The creators are humans living like everyone else so they are influenced by the present, watching their creations as soon as possible is ideal. I pick the best films of the year by various criteria like imdb ratings, googling best films of the year lists, awards, artists I trust etc.

The second direction is older films and it's more complex. I started as a kid by watching films on TV which usually meant going back max 30 years, in genres I enjoyed the most like actions films. When internet bandwidth increased, I found the imdb top 250 film list. Then I found the theyshootpictures 1000 greatest films list that I went though. Now I make mini projects of my own, like the silent era, I made a list of the best silent films and watched them chronologically. Other projects have been music films or specific arists like Renoir and Mads Mikkelsen.

My approach for past films started kind of random then more structured and that's what I would advise. Start by watching past films from recent decades/directors/genres you most enjoy so as to build a strong base. When you feel ready for it, go even further in the past, explore directors that are famous but you don't know so much, genres that aren't your favourites but not your worst either. Finally, go for very old films up to eventually the silent era, directors recognised for their artistry but also difficulty (like Angelopoulos), genres that you dislike. 

**Random tips:**  

* I try giving all films my full attention, putting the mobile phone away is ideal.   
* Never consume trailers or any material regarding a film you might watch. The less you know, the better. Even the film's duration and actors can be spoilers in a sense. It requires a lot of self-discipline but there are so many positive surprises this way.
* I rarely ever watch films a second time. That's not because I remember them perfectly or that I find no value in rewatching films. It's just that I want to experience as many new films as possible. When I get older however, I plan to rewatch the ones I enjoyed the most.  
* The more self-imposed restrictions you get rid of, the better. Genres and artists you dislike, taboo or triggering subjects etc.
* Don't get discouraged when you find yourself in a phase where you don't want to watch films! The desire will probably come back after a while, especially if you mix up things with reading books, listening to music, playing games etc.   
* Once fully committed to a subject, chronological order, whilst monotonous, is more rewarding.  
* Be your own curator, avoid the algorithms.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >The more self-imposed restrictions you get rid of, the better. 

For someone who says that, you certainly have a lot of self-imposed restrictions! Chronological order is more rewarding? Watching films closest to their release date is better? No second viewings? Mastery of technical aspects is at its peak now compared to the past? Those all sound completely arbitrary to me. 

My approach is just a basic "what do I most feel like watching based on what's available right now?" I don't worry too much about seeing the "best" films. Instead I try to find something to enjoy in whatever I watch. This weekend it was Freaky Friday & Freakier Friday because we had friends with teenagers visiting, and we figured that they were something we'd all like. I never would have watched them if I'd been on my own, but I enjoyed them both very much. 

Ultimately we watch films for enjoyment. Having rules turns it into a process to be optimised. You are right about not watching trailers and putting your phone away though. :-)
2. I choose films in four main ways. The first, for modern films, is a simple matter of judgment: Does this look like a movie I want to see? The second is recommendations; generally this is word of mouth from friends, although I have an advantage in that my wife is enormously well-watched and has good taste, but algorithmic recommendations can be useful too. Recommendations can impact modern films too; I think my judgment is usually pretty good, but if I dismissed a film that my friends told me they loved, I might check it out after all. Third, I seek out movies made by directors I like (or actors, but usually directors), or movies similar to movies I liked. Writers can be good to follow too, but because their work can be changed so much during production, it isn't always as reliable as you might think. Fourth, curation can be helpful, whether it comes from a distributor like Criterion or something like a "best of" list. 
  - > algorithmic recommendations can be useful too

Streaming platforms or websites? From discussions with friends using streaming platforms and some browsing I have done on their accounts, it seems very underwhelming. More like shoehorning you to what type of viewer they want you as than expanding your horizons.
3. To be completely honest? 

By not being a film snob and just watch everything I thonk I might like without attention to what other people in the community might consider "good" or "real" films, although it usually largely overlaps. 

Also films friends with similar tastes recommend and what runs in the arthouse and mainstream cinema in my city. We have quite a lot of cinemas here and a lot of film festivals all year round.

If I am not sure about whether I like something I just look it up on Rotten Tomatoes. But not for the scores (it has to be below 20% on both aisles for me become a somewhat relevant information) but because the short summaries of critics' reviews is a pretty good way to form minimal expectation but not too much expectatiom nor to spoil yourself. Also short teasers are also great.

And I'll do it the other way around: I basically rewatch all films I reasonably liked as to appreciate them from a new perspective.

And a no phone policy is not ideal - it is compulsory.
  - >By not being a film snob and just watch everything I thonk I might like without attention to what other people in the community might consider "good" or "real" films, although it usually largely overlaps. 

Definitely! The problem with only following the critical consensus is that you aren't really exploring or developing your own taste. It's very limiting if you only watch things you've already heard are good. It can also lead to a view that anything not in the best-of lists is somehow inferior. All it really means is that fewer people like those films, but they could still have something that you really connect with.

It's also a myth that you can only learn about films from watching the best ones. There's plenty to learn from how NOT to do it as well, and it makes the best ones seem even better.
4. For movies coming out, I try to avoid as much information as possible, I barely watch trailers either. If a trailer introduces me to an interesting-looking movie then I try to avoid hearing anything else about it. 

For movies that are on physical media/streaming services, I don't worry too much about completing a particular list of films (e.g. imdb top 250, letterboxd top 500.) I have an awareness of writers, directors, and actors that I should check out to broaden my palette, as well as an awareness of the writers, directors, and actors I personally admire. As I watch more movies, I learn of more names, movements, etc. 

I usually watch 1-2 movies a week. I personally like to sit with a movie and chew on it for a while.

At this particular moment in my life, I usually get movies from my library, and I try to keep a decent balance of years and language. I also make sure that at least one of my checkouts is an animated film. 

While I'm still growing as someone who engages with film (and as a person overall) I gravitate to simple, yet compelling stories told well and I generally seek out films that adhere to that. I do try to get out of my comfort zone and take a chance which can be fun! I didn't think I would enjoy Blue Velvet or Y Tu Mama Tambien but I ended up quite liking them. 

Phones and other distractions should be as far away as possible. That should be a given for intentional art consumption. 
5. Letterboxd: What have best general ratings that I haven’t alr seen, and did my friends enjoy it. Most of the time I exclude them if my friends didn’t enjoy it (even if it has a high general rating). Then I have some where I read the description and the plot/theme of the movie intrests me. 
6. I read and try and pick out the best movies, directors to get familiar with. Started a French New Wave book. I haven't been able to focus myself on any country or movement, or decades, I like to flit around, and watch popular movies and arthouse, and cast wide nets. I avoid many movies outside of my taste profile, just the top ones in genres I'm not into. I am finding that I like to watch some movies over and over again when I'm tired, and need familiarity, or the commentary is interesting. I watched Yojimbo and then the commentary right after, back to back. I do sort on Letterboxd by highest ranking, and add to my watchlist, but I don't really look at my watch list. I created a document with top directors and movies I hadn't seen and I recently crossed out the ones I'd seen. I mostly follow my instincts and moods while cruising for movies, I can't stay disciplined and watch one director or one movement, I flit around. I watch movies most days, but sometimes I watch TV shows, and post on Serialized. I'm trying to develop myself as a critic, articulate, develop my writing skills.
